an7.29:1.1:
"Bhikkhus, these seven things lead to the decline of a lay follower. What seven? (1) He stops seeing bhikkhus; (2) he neglects listening to the good Dhamma; (3) he does not train in the higher virtuous behavior; (4) he is full of suspicion toward bhikkhus, whether they be elders, newly ordained, or of middle standing; (5) he listens to the Dhamma with a mind bent on criticism, seeking to find faults with it; (6) he seeks a person worthy of offerings among outsiders; (7) he first does
deeds there.
These seven things lead to the decline of a lay follower.
an7.29:1.2:
"Bhikkhus, these seven things lead to the non-decline of a lay follower. What seven? (1) He does not stop seeing bhikkhus; (2) he does not neglect listening to the good Dhamma; (3) he trains in the higher virtuous behavior; (4) he is full of confidence in bhikkhus, whether they be elders, newly ordained, or of middle standing; (5) he listens to the Dhamma with a mind that is not bent on criticism, not seeking to find faults with it; (6) he does not seek a person worthy of offerings among outsiders; (7) he first does
deeds here. These seven things lead to the non-decline of a lay follower."
an7.29:1.3:
The lay follower who stops seeing
(bhikkhus) who have developed themselves,
hearing the noble ones’ teachings,
and does not train in the higher virtue;
an7.29:1.5:
with a mind bent on criticism;
the lay follower who seeks
one worthy of offerings among outsiders
and first undertakes to do
meritorious deeds toward them:
an7.29:1.6:
these seven well-taught principles
describe what leads to decline.
A lay follower who resorts to them
falls away from the good Dhamma.
an7.29:1.7:
The lay follower who does not stop seeing
who have developed themselves,
who listens to the noble ones’ teachings,
and trains in the higher virtue;
an7.29:1.9:
the lay follower who seeks none
worthy of offerings among outsiders;
but who here first undertakes
to do meritorious deeds:
an7.29:1.10:
these seven well-taught principles
describe what leads to non-decline.
A lay follower who resorts to them
does not fall away from the good Dhamma.
